revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]][[es:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]][[fa:گونگن-زوکوری]][[fr:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]][[ja:権現造]][[pl:Gongen-zukuri]][[pt:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]][[ru:Гонгэн-дзукури]]
{{Short description|Architectural style of Shintoist shrine}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|right|thumb|A gongen-zukuri shrine. From the top: ''honden'', ''ishi-no-ma'', ''haiden''. In yellow the ridges of the roofs]] {{nihongo|'''''Ishi-no-ma-zukuri'''''|石の間造}}, also called {{nihongo||権現造|[[gongen]]-zukuri}}, {{nihongo|''yatsumune-zukuri''|八棟造}} and {{nihongo|''miyadera-zukuri''|宮寺造}}, is a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the ''[[honden]]'', or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 2, 2009</ref>
The connecting passage can be called {{nihongo|''ai-no-ma''|相の間|}}, {{nihongo|''ishi-no-ma''|石の間}}, or {{nihongo|''chūden''|中殿}} ("intermediate hall").<ref name="Jaa"/> The floor of each of the three halls can be at a different level. If the ''ai-no-ma'' is paved with stones it is called ''ishi-no-ma'', whence the name of the style. It can, however, be paved with planks or ''[[tatami]]''. Its width is often the same as the ''honden's'', with the ''haiden'' from one to three ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'' wider.<ref name="Jaa"/>
This style, rather than the structure of a building, defines the relationship between member structures of a shrine. Each member then belongs to a particular {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}. For example, the ''honden'' and ''haiden'' at {{nihongo|Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine|大崎八幡宮|Ōsaki Hachiman-gū}} are single-storied, ''irimoya-zukuri'' edifices.<ref>{{cite web | url = http://www.okos.co.jp/oosaki/img/english.pdf | title = Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine - Information in English | publisher = [[Ōsaki Hachiman Shrine]] | access-date = 2009-dd-04 }}</ref> Because they are connected by a passage called ''ishi-no-ma'' and are covered by a single roof, however, the complex is classified as belonging to the ''ishi-no-ma-zukuri'' style.
One of the oldest examples is [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.<ref name="sos2"/> The ''gongen-zukuri'' name comes from {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|qid=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, which enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen ({{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}) and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on December 5, 2009</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
